saying,

Now, external to that, in 2024, it was an explosive year for us.
We went from the blog driving maybe 6,000 to 7,000 organic visits to we're now at 32,000 organic visits, you know, a month.
And then you add email and direct and everything else, you know, it's a pretty substantial amount.

the total traffic.

We are more present in a lot of different places.

your question of like, well, how do we ideate this thing?

into a reality.

think it's because 2025 has been a really tough year from an SEO perspective with the AI overviews and the LLMs.

And so where our click-through rate was like 2.4%, it's now 0.8%.
It's an absolute beast.
beast.
And so what I'm actively trying to do, and I spent actually the last two months is building a playbook for 2026 for how do we win an AI search?
How does AirDNA win an AI search?

The good news is that because we have now over 600 pieces of content that we've produced, and it kind of spans a ton of different topics around the short-term rental industry, we do get cited a lot, and our referral traffic from LLMs has grown.
But it's sort of plateaued, and I think generally there's a sentiment of like WTF, like what are we going to do?
Like how are we going to tackle this thing?
And it's both, for me it's really exciting, but it is also a bit stressful because nobody really has a clear answer, like where it's a lot of experimentation.
And I think that's now really the biggest thing that I've taken from this is the old playbook is kind of a little dead in the sense of you used to really work very hard on the blog.
That was kind of your baseline.
And then once the blog was on

I'm enjoying that a lot.

All right, so then how about we add multiple answers to prompts in one larger piece, and then it'll understand.

it takes, I'm actually quite proud of this one because I spent a lot of time in this one exact workflow.

mean, which are their English.
And so you want to be able to create an experience that feels really natural to them.
So I created an AI workflow that doesn't just take the content and then like spit it out into the other language.
The first part of the AI workflow is actually first a, it's a checker of, is, should we even create this content for this market and for this culture?
And also legally and like regulation wise, like, does it make sense?
And so it'll go through a deep check to see all of those things.
So for instance, rental arbitrage, which is basically where you, do you know what rental arbitrage is?
I have some hunches, but I'll just explain it in like a second.
Rental arbitrage is I rent an apartment for $2,000 and then I leave and stay somewhere else and I Airbnb the place.
Yeah, yeah, yeah.
Right.
So that's, that's not appropriate in certain countries.
And so when you put it through that filter, it says, ah, this is not culturally appropriate, or this is not appropriate because of X,
Then it takes the piece and it writes the entire article optimized for that keyword in the local language.

Then it compares the optimized piece to the English piece to see what did we miss, what pieces of data are interesting from the original piece, and then let's integrate it, and then it produces the final piece.
That's super cool.

In terms of, you were talking a little bit about, you know, how you think about bottom, top, top funnel, bottom funnel, as you're thinking about kind of,
Differentiating or segmenting your strategy in a sense, but how do you think about balancing the short and the longer term as you're doing this planning?

You're leaning into how to win more in AI visibility.
How do you think about building those plans and those key horizons so you're not too short-term focused, but also not focusing too much in the long term at the same time?
Yeah.

For me, I've gone through a lot of different iterations on this at this company and other companies, but what I've really come down to recently that I think is a really strong strategy is, broadly speaking, content and SEO is, in its nature, a long-term strategy.
You could say, yes, you have some exceptions where a piece starts to rank within a couple weeks or maybe a month, and it starts to drive some traffic, but you're still talking about peanuts.
It's not like, oh, I'm going to turn on the paid engine, put $50,000 into it, and then immediately...

But what you can do, and what we've now started to implement a lot more, this is actually something I just started two months ago and has been working really well, is thinking about content as like a broader, what is the broader usage?

And so now I'm coordinating much more deeply with email, with paid, with PR, you know, all these different, even with the product team itself, if there's a way for us to integrate that content within the product experience, how do we do that?
And that allows us to create, a perfect example for this is we launched another programmatic play about three months ago.
It was a top Airbnb by state.
So it's 50 programmatic pages.
And so you would be like top Airbnb market in Texas, for instance.
And then it gives you like the 18 top markets in that place.
So we created that content and then it's doing pretty good organically.
And I was like, this feels like a waste.
Like we should be doing so much more with this.
And so then we integrated and we built a.
The email campaign that was segmented by the user, what state they were in, and then we launched this to them, and it was a huge success.
And we drove a ton of conversions from doing that because then it felt so specific to them.
So that for me is like, if you want immediate short-term results, it's let's leverage these other own distribution channels that you have.
And of course, like if you can integrate with influencer marketing even better, right?
Then you're getting, you're fishing in other people's ponds and not just your own.
Yeah, love that.
Anytime we have a client who has some sort of video play, whatever that may be, I'm trying to see, you know, if we can get compound effects from that content.
Similarly is kind of how you were breaking that down.
So like one of things I've done in the past pre-AI even was, you know, a common tactic.
If you have like an on-demand webinar, you probably work those in your nurture streams in some way or another.
You may send people directly to the thank you page because they're already in your nurture stream.
So you're not going to ask them to submit again.
And it's like, you know, generally an okay experience, but probably can be better.

And so for our customers, you've already created this thing.
We're not asking you to do anything else, really.
We can then lengthen the distribution of that and hopefully get some SEO value from that.
But even, maybe there is, maybe there isn't, but you can also then work that into your email nurture streams as well.
Like work those articles into it.
And that's going to be a better user experience than sending someone straight to a thank you page.
And so it's a similar version of like that same mindset that you're talking about.
It's like, how can you take these things, repurpose in a whole variety of ways?

sure that I kind of briefed...

then you have your fan out prompts.

Chris, just to learn a bit more about your thinking around that.

Yeah, so I'm the director of organic growth, which means that it's, I also, on my team, I have affiliate marketing, I have influencer marketing, social media, kind of like the whole gamut of anything that's organic.
And so, like, with that in mind, obviously, like, I've done a ton of research, I've done webinars, love the GrowthX webinars, by the way, that's, like, what brought me to this conversation.

But it's clear that it's not just blogs, it's really clear, like, there's statistics that I've seen recently, actually, was a webinar by Pavilion, that I thought was really good, that said, like, it was like 26% or something is blog of the stuff that's cited in the internet.

I don't know if that's a fully factual, but it was still a big enough that I was like, yeah, no, it's, it's obviously very significant, because it's long form content, it's usually very high quality, it's answering the question they have, the LLM wants quantity, it wants to be able to have a lot to be able to digest.
Yeah, so.
So for sure, that's a big piece of it.
But right beside it is YouTube.
Right beside that is Reddit.
And so we're thinking a lot about, okay, what other things can we experiment with those other channels?
We did a big project where we created over the last six months, 109 YouTube videos that are all based off of our top performing blogs.
And that's starting to actually really help us, I think, broadly speaking.
It's a little harder to track, but I know the rising tides, right?
It's starting to have that impact.
Reddit is very hard to tackle.
That's one where I don't know how to tackle it, but it's really in the back of my mind.
So to go back to your question, I'm not bullish about it being the traditional blog format.
I don't think that's true.
I think that the LLMs, at its core, its prerogative is, let me find the best answer to this question.
And so is it always, of course.
Blog format?
No, definitely not.
There's a lot of blogs that are a bunch of fluff and don't give any answer.
Or in some cases, you'll see that it'll cite something that on Google is the 27th result, and it ignores the first three.
And it's because it just has a better way of answering it in a concise way.
so, yeah, sorry, did I answer your question?
hope I No, I mean, honestly, it's kind of one of those things.
There's not really a right or wrong answer, differing perspectives.
To your point earlier, like, there's a lot for us all to figure out right now, and there isn't just some silver bullet.

And, you know, I think the slight nuance that we may see, like, a bit differently on this, but, like, is, like, up for debate in a lot of ways is, like, I think the slight difference in our framing and how we think about it here is, you mentioned something to the fact of, like, comprehensiveness of answer.
I think that is true to a degree, perhaps even.
And if you figure out some sort of like tie in from like an authoritative perspective to like understand if this is a legit answer or not.
But I think the thing that's important to come back to is like also these LLMs are inherently lazy because that's what they're built to do, right?
And so like they need to be able to try to figure out how to get to the best answer as efficiently as possible.
And so what does that then look like, though, as you're trying to put together this recipe of all the right things to get that forward?
And it's a, yeah, I mean, clearly no one has just completely unpacked that.
There's probably, it's probably because there isn't, you know, a clear system to really do that consistently.
But generally, I think we're, you know, certainly aligned is like, you know, being really mindful of who the ICP is, what are their questions, and how do you create really good answers to that?
Whatever that looks like, right?
I do think we probably skew more blog oriented.
And how we're tackling it today and certainly finding some value, but I do think we probably need to identify patterns of when, how should we think about when we don't do that?
